text: The surgeon observes the right colon, which is very mobile and loosely attached to the abdominal wall by a wide band known as the lead band. The aim is to mobilize the colon to the left side by dividing this band.

The surgeon elevates the transverse colon, revealing the absence of the duodenum crossing the midline. The aorta and vena cava are fully exposed, indicating complete malrotation. The lead band is divided, and the cecum is mobilized to the left upper quadrant. An appendectomy is performed as part of the procedure.

The surgeon mobilizes the cecum to the left side of the abdomen, creating a widened base of the mesentery, with the final goal of positioning the small bowel on the right side and the large bowel on the left side.

The surgeon identifies a variation of intestinal malrotation where the entire small bowel is enclosed in a peritoneal sac, resembling a paraduodenal hernia. The small bowel is carefully extracted from this sac.

The surgeon navigates proximally along the small bowel to reach the duodenal-jejunal junction, located on the right side of the abdomen. The peritoneal sac enclosing the small bowel is excised to prevent re-herniation.
The surgeon spreads the mesentery to widen it, extending from the right abdomen to the left upper abdomen. The cecum is then fixed to the left abdominal wall using a few non-absorbable stitches.
